narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 63.5% chance that you would not pass. This prediction stems from a careful analysis of key elements in your profile, like your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, LSAT score of 36, Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles, family income in the middle bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. The model flagged your Year 3 academic decile of 1 as the strongest factor pushing toward a failure prediction, since it sits well below the average of around 6 for students who passed the bar—suggesting your later undergraduate performance raised significant concerns about sustaining the rigor needed for law school and the exam. Your Year 1 decile of 2 also contributed in the same direction, though less forcefully, as it too falls short of the typical 6 seen among successful bar takers. On a more positive note, your LSAT score worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success because it's close to the 37.27 average for those who passed, showing solid potential in logical reasoning skills. While your middle family income aligns with over a third of bar passers and your full-time status matches nearly all of them, these didn't outweigh the academic decile concerns in the model's eyes. Compared to admitted students, whose profiles generally show stronger grade deciles and slightly higher GPAs and LSATs, your application stood out as riskier for bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your character or background—gender and race weren't factors here—but a data-driven call to protect the program's outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score or strengthening your academic record through a post-baccalaureate program or additional coursework that could improve your decile rankings. Gathering stronger letters of recommendation highlighting your resilience, especially in later years, might also help reframe your story. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these gaps—reach out to admissions for advice on reapplying with an updated profile, and keep pursuing your goals with the determination that's gotten you this far.
